metrics.narrative: The buyer articulated qualitative operational pain but has not quantified financial impact or ROI targets. On the 22 Jun 2026 call, Ming described struggling to run sourcing events, intake inefficiencies, and siloed point solutions across the S2P stack (separate contracts, P2P, analytics tools). Quote: 'Struggling to run events, intake, sourcing and ANA were other areas of interest' (Next_Step__c, updated 22 Jun). The operational-excellence team and P2P directors (Fernando for NA, Omar for EU) are aware of process friction but no cost/savings/cycle-time metrics have been stated. Ming's AI adoption strategy pitch to the CPO (~8 Jun) presumably included a business case, but the content and any quantified value have not been shared with the relevant stakeholder deal is pre-commercial, so no ROI workshop or value-case co-build has occurred. Quantifying the pain (event cycle time, intake backlog, contract leakage, spend under management) and tying it to Veolia's integration ROI story is the immediate move to create a credible business case, but it cannot proceed without EB engagement and budget unfreezing.

sources: Discovery calls 25 May, 3 Jun, 22 Jun 2026 (Ming Li)
Known stakeholders and risks from prior sweep
champion.narrative: Ming Li (North America Strategic Sourcing lead) is the confirmed champion, active and engaged, but organizationally siloed with weak executive/commercial influence. Ming has been the sole Zycus advocate since initial contact (25 May call, follow-up 3 Jun, discovery 4 Jun rescheduled to later in Jun, latest call 22 Jun). He built an AI adoption strategy presentation and pitched it to the CPO around 8 Jun 2026, demonstrating initiative and internal advocacy. He invited operational stakeholders (Josh from operational-excellence, Fernando and Omar from P2P) into the conversation, showing multi-threading effort. However, Ming has no budget authority, no Economic Buyer access, and limited influence over the operational-excellence/digital-tools team (Josh) that controls vendor decisions. Quote from Ming on 22 Jun: 'I'm not involve other all other technology stuff... they are the backend people'. He is a strong operational champion (understands the pain, willing to push internally, maintains engagement with Zycus) but a weak political champion (cannot unlock budget, cannot escalate to the CPO, cannot override the integration freeze). The CPO has not responded to Ming's pitch after 30 days, signaling either low priority or Ming's lack of influence at the executive level. Ming's engagement is active (last contact 22 Jun, 16 days ago, still recent), but without a commercial/executive co-champion or direct EB sponsorship, his advocacy cannot move the deal forward. He is a developing champion at risk of cooling if the freeze persists and Zycus disengages.

competition.narrative: Competing primarily against SAP Ariba (the incumbent) and against internal inertia during the Suez-Veolia integration freeze. Ariba is Veolia's current S2P platform (confirmed 22 Jun Next_Step: 'Ariba customer today'), and the operational-excellence team (Josh) is actively testing Ariba's AI/RFQ automation capabilities as the default path before considering new vendors. Quote from Ming on 22 Jun: 'they are trying to see what Ariba can come out with first'. So far, Ming reports, 'haven't seen any user case success', meaning Ariba's AI pilot has not yet delivered compelling results—this is Zycus's window. However, if Ariba delivers a passable AI/automation capability during the 1-2 year integration freeze, inertia ('good enough' incumbent vs new vendor procurement process) may block Zycus adoption. A second competitor is the unknown future ERP vendor: Veolia is consolidating Suez's ERP/tools stack, and the future ERP platform (SAP, Oracle, Workday, other) is undecided. If the future ERP includes a bundled procurement suite, that could displace both Ariba and Zycus. The competitive wedge: Zycus's Merlin/Agentic AI for sourcing/negotiation/intake automation vs Ariba's incumbent position and integration inertia. How we win: demonstrate a live P2P/S2P integration with SAP (or the future ERP) that delivers measurably better AI-driven automation (event cycle time, supplier negotiation intelligence, intake deflection) than Ariba's pilot, AND secure a parallel pilot carve-out (e.g. North America category sourcing or a single business unit) that bypasses the enterprise freeze and creates a reference win DURING the integration period, so when the tools decision reopens post-integration, Zycus is the proven incumbent alternative, not the new vendor. The activation: position the pilot as integration-friendly (API/middleware, no ERP replacement, de-risked scope) and tie it to the relevant stakeholder adoption roadmap so the CPO sees parallel progress, not disruption.

identify_pain.narrative: The pain is confirmed and owned by the relevant stakeholder strategic sourcing (Ming Li) and regional P2P operations (Fernando NA, Omar EU), but it has not been quantified or escalated to executive urgency. Ming described on 22 Jun: struggling to run sourcing events (RFx cycle inefficiency), intake bottlenecks (iRequest scope), and a fragmented S2P tech stack with separate point solutions for contracts, P2P, analytics, creating process friction and data silos. Quote from Next_Step (22 Jun): 'Ariba customer today, multiple other point solutions used for each area of S2P including a separate contracts solution. Struggling to run events, intake, sourcing and ANA were other areas of interest'. The pain is operational (sourcing/P2P teams manually stitching together workflows across disconnected tools) rather than financial crisis (no stated compliance failure, audit finding, or cost overrun forcing immediate action). The operational-excellence team (Josh) and the European Operational Excellence Team (6-7 people, unnamed) are aware of the pain but have prioritized the Suez integration over addressing it with a new vendor. The pain is real and confirmed, but it is not urgent enough to override the integration freeze, and no executive (CPO, CFO, CIO) has made it a burning platform. The pain owner is Ming and the P2P directors, but they lack the authority to act on it.

paper_process.narrative: No contracting, procurement, legal, or InfoSec mechanics have been discussed because the deal has not reached commercial stages. No MSA, SOW, security review, or ERP/systems integration scoping has occurred. The buyer's current priority is the Suez-Veolia ERP/tools consolidation, a 1-2 year integration program that will define the future procurement tech stack and determine whether Zycus (or any new vendor) fits. Ming stated on 22 Jun: 'we had to wait until net happen, and then after net, we might be able to try to push your AI stuff into the agenda'. This suggests the paper process cannot even BEGIN until the integration decision is made and a future ERP platform is selected (currently unknown). No partner/SI involvement has been mentioned. No contract expiration date is forcing urgency. The only visibility into paper process will come from: (1) EB engagement to understand Veolia's procurement/legal approval chain, or (2) a parallel pilot carve-out that bypasses the full enterprise procurement process during the freeze. Neither path is active.

economic_buyer.narrative: The Economic Buyer is the CPO (Paris-based, unnamed), who controls global procurement tool budgets and commercial decisions for Veolia. Ming Li submitted an AI adoption strategy presentation to the CPO around 8 Jun 2026, but as of the 22 Jun call (30 days later), the CPO has given no response, no budget approval, and no mandate to advance. Quote from Ming on 22 Jun: 'they didn't approve any advancement budget... only for the integration' and 'we had to wait until net happen, and then after net, we might be able to try to push your AI stuff into the agenda'. The CPO is passive or unengaged; Ming has no direct access and cannot escalate. The operational-excellence team (Josh) and regional P2P directors (Fernando, Omar) are engaged but cannot approve budget—they are influencers, not the EB. Without EB sponsorship, the deal has no commercial path. The evidence shows budget authority sits with the CPO, but engagement is completely absent, and Ming is blocked from reaching them.

decision_process.narrative: The decision process is visible in outline but stalled at the gate. The buyer's governance model has three layers: (1) North America strategic sourcing (Ming Li, operational reach but no budget authority), (2) operational-excellence/digital-tools team (Josh, controls vendor evaluation and tool roadmap, testing Ariba AI in parallel), and (3) the CPO (Paris, budget approval and commercial sign-off, currently unengaged). The process the buyer is running: Ming pitches AI adoption strategy to the CPO → CPO approval gates budget → operational-excellence team launches vendor evaluation (RFI/RFP, Ariba vs Zycus vs others) → shortlist and demos → regional P2P directors (Fernando NA, Omar EU) and category leads validate fit → commercial negotiation → contract. However, this process is frozen at step 1: the CPO has not approved advancement budget, and the operational-excellence team is testing Ariba as the default incumbent path before opening a competitive evaluation. Ming stated on 22 Jun: 'they didn't approve any advancement budget... only for the integration' and 'we had to wait until net happen'. The Suez-Veolia integration is the explicit gate; no new tool decision can proceed until integration completes (1-2 year timeline). The call sequence shows no escalation, no executive join, no procurement/legal involvement—only discovery-level operational conversations with Ming, Josh, Fernando, Omar. The process exists but is not active.

decision_criteria.narrative: The buyer's evaluation criteria are partially visible from the product scope and stated pain points but have not been formalized into an RFP, RFI, or BRD. The Next_Step field (22 Jun) lists areas of interest: struggling to run sourcing events, intake automation, end-to-end S2P workflow (request, PR, PO, invoice, supplier interaction), and analytics (ANA). Products in scope per Salesforce: iContract, iSource, iRequest, Merlin, Agentic AI, ANA—a comprehensive suite. Ming's requirements from prior calls: demonstrate Zycus integrates with SAP (not replacing it) in a live P2P customer example, and show end-to-end workflow without requiring ERP replacement. The operational-excellence team is also evaluating criteria around AI/RFQ automation (testing Ariba's capabilities in parallel). However, no formal evaluation framework, scoring rubric, or decision-maker sign-off on criteria has occurred because the deal is frozen pre-RFI. The criteria that matter are inferred (integration ease, AI/automation ROI, minimal ERP disruption during the Suez consolidation), not confirmed in a structured process. Formalizing criteria requires EB engagement and an RFI/RFP launch, neither of which can happen under the current budget freeze.

north_star_verdict.headline: Deal is structurally blocked by a 1-2 year ERP/tools integration freeze with no approved budget, no Economic Buyer engagement, and no credible path to the Oct-26 close date. Champion Ming is organizationally siloed with no commercial authority. The buyer explicitly stated no 2026 advancement budget exists beyond Suez integration spend, and the CPO has not responded to Ming's AI pitch after 30 days. Veolia is evaluating incumbent Ariba's AI capabilities as the default path before considering new vendors. Without executive sponsorship, a parallel pilot carve-out, or a realistic 2027-2028 timeline, this deal will remain dormant indefinitely.

flags: Champion multi-thread: Ming Li is a strong, active champion (AI lead, sourcing director) but has NO budget authority or EB access. Josh (North America procurement digital lead, tool/budget owner) is the critical co-champion to cultivate—he controls the operational-excellence team's vendor decisions and has direct line to the CPO/exec team. Fernando (P2P director) and Omar (regional buyer manager) are influential P2P pain owners who can advocate for a pilot. Build a coalition: arm all four with a concise, CPO-ready pilot business case (quantified P2P impact, ERP-safe architecture, 90-day proof-of-value) so when the budget gate opens they can jointly sponsor it. Single-thread risk is HIGH if Ming alone carries this through a 1-2 year freeze.

Pilot-first wedge against the integration freeze: The buyer's stated objection is 'we need to complete the Suez integration and decide the ERP platform first, THEN consider new tools' (1-2 year timeline). Challenge that sequencing with a pilot-first approach: a narrow, high-impact P2P pilot (on-time payment 75%→95%, supplier onboarding 20-30d→1wk) can run IN PARALLEL to the integration because Zycus is ERP-complementary (no SAP replacement required) and modular (deploy P2P independently, expand post-integration). Frame it as 'validate the AI ROI now on a contained use case so you can confidently scale it across the consolidated organization later, rather than wait 2 years and start from zero'. This is the ONLY path to pull the close date forward; otherwise, requalify to 2027-2028 pipeline. Position the pilot as a strategic hedge—if Ariba's automation pilots fail (as they have so far), Veolia needs a proven alternative ready to deploy when the integration completes.

Competitive displacement strategy vs Ariba: Ariba is the incumbent with low adoption and no successful AI pilots yet, but they have the operational-excellence team's attention by default ('let's see what Ariba can do first'). Our counter: build a direct comparison narrative—'Ariba is a point-solution bolt-on with limited AI depth; Zycus is the comprehensive, AI-native source-to-pay platform with proven P2P automation (share 2-3 customer proof points: on-time payment improvement, supplier onboarding acceleration, tail-spend savings via ANA). Ariba will incrementally patch; Zycus leapfrogs.' Share this narrative WITH Ming/Josh as a Leave-Behind they can use internally, and offer a bake-off pilot: 'Run a 90-day Zycus P2P pilot alongside Ariba's RFQ automation test; measure which delivers measurable operational improvement.' This forces a data-driven decision and neutralizes the incumbent advantage. If Ariba's pilots continue to underwhelm, we become the obvious choice when budget reopens.

Single-thread vulnerability: Ming is the sole active champion with strong operational reach but no budget authority, no EB access, and limited influence over the operational-excellence/digital team (Josh) that controls vendor decisions. Ming is traveling to Asia for two months starting late June 2026. If Ming is the only thread, the deal will stall. Convert Josh (North America procurement digital lead), Fernando (North America P2P director), and Omar (regional buyer manager) into co-champions who can advocate for pilot budget and executive briefing.

Zero buyer-side Contact Roles are logged on this opportunity despite four named engaged stakeholders (Ming Li, Josh, Fernando, Omar) across three calls; add them to multi-thread the record and support EB tracking.

SAP Ariba is being tested internally as the default RFQ/sourcing path with 'no user case success yet' as of 22 Jun 2026; reinforce Zycus's agentic AI differentiation now, before Ariba shows any progress.

Champion Ming Li has no budget authority and is structurally siloed from the operational-excellence/digital-tools team (Josh) that controls vendor decisions; building a second champion in Josh or Fernando is the highest-leverage lever to survive the 1-2 year ERP integration freeze.

Convert Ming Li's one quantifiable ask (on-time payment 75% -> 95%) into a scoped mini business case he can hand the CPO directly, instead of pitching the full suite; he explicitly said one proven win would be 'very convincing to our CPO' (4 Jun 2026).
Get direct access to Josh, Fernando, and Omar, who attended the 17 Jun 2026 demo and hold the technical/budget visibility Ming Li lacks as a siloed sourcing lead.

Track the SAP Ariba parallel pilot (no user-case success as of 22 Jun 2026); keep fresh proof points ready so a 'good enough incumbent' result doesn't close the door before Zycus is formally evaluated.
best_practice_check.summary: Deal fails multiple critical best practices: no Economic Buyer engagement (CPO silent for 30 days), single-threaded on a champion with no budget authority, no pricing/commercial discussion, and a close date 12-24 months ahead of the buyer's stated timeline. The 1-2 year integration freeze blocks progression, and no parallel pilot path has been secured. Incumbent Ariba is being tested as the default, creating competitive urgency.

competitive_position.summary: Competing against SAP Ariba (incumbent, actively testing AI/RFQ automation as default path) and against the 1-2 year Suez-Veolia integration freeze that blocks any new vendor approval. Ariba has integration inertia but has not yet delivered a successful AI use case per Ming. Zycus's wedge is superior Merlin/Agentic AI automation, but the window to prove it is narrow and requires a parallel pilot carve-out to create a reference win during the freeze. If Ariba delivers passable AI during the freeze, inertia may block Zycus. Unknown future ERP vendor is a secondary competitor if the ERP includes bundled procurement.

Pilot-first wedge against the integration freeze: The buyer's stated objection is 'we need to complete the Suez integration and decide the ERP platform first, THEN consider new tools' (1-2 year timeline). Challenge that sequencing with a pilot-first approach: a narrow, high-impact P2P pilot (on-time payment 75%→95%, supplier onboarding 20-30d→1wk) can run IN PARALLEL to the integration because Zycus is ERP-complementary (no SAP replacement required) and modular (deploy P2P independently, expand post-integration). Frame it as 'validate the AI ROI now on a contained use case so you can confidently scale it across the consolidated organization later, rather than wait 2 years and start from zero'. This is the ONLY path to pull the close date forward; otherwise, requalify to 2027-2028 pipeline. Position the pilot as a strategic hedge—if Ariba's automation pilots fail (as they have so far), Veolia needs a proven alternative ready to deploy when the integration completes.

Competitive displacement strategy vs Ariba: Ariba is the incumbent with low adoption and no successful AI pilots yet, but they have the operational-excellence team's attention by default ('let's see what Ariba can do first'). Our counter: build a direct comparison narrative—'Ariba is a point-solution bolt-on with limited AI depth; Zycus is the comprehensive, AI-native source-to-pay platform with proven P2P automation (share 2-3 customer proof points: on-time payment improvement, supplier onboarding acceleration, tail-spend savings via ANA). Ariba will incrementally patch; Zycus leapfrogs.' Share this narrative WITH Ming/Josh as a Leave-Behind they can use internally, and offer a bake-off pilot: 'Run a 90-day Zycus P2P pilot alongside Ariba's RFQ automation test; measure which delivers measurable operational improvement.' This forces a data-driven decision and neutralizes the incumbent advantage. If Ariba's pilots continue to underwhelm, we become the obvious choice when budget reopens.

deltas.subject: Champion multi-thread: Ming Li is a strong, active champion (AI lead, sourcing director) but has NO budget authority or EB access. Josh (North America procurement digital lead, tool/budget owner) is the critical co-champion to cultivate—he controls the operational-excellence team's vendor decisions and has direct line to the CPO/exec team. Fernando (P2P director) and Omar (regional buyer manager) are influential P2P pain owners who can advocate for a pilot. Build a coalition: arm all four with a concise, CPO-ready pilot business case (quantified P2P impact, ERP-safe architecture, 90-day proof-of-value) so when the budget gate opens they can jointly sponsor it. Single-thread risk is HIGH if Ming alone carries this through a 1-2 year freeze.

deltas.to: Pilot-first wedge against the integration freeze: The buyer's stated objection is 'we need to complete the Suez integration and decide the ERP platform first, T
deltas.subject: Pilot-first wedge against the integration freeze: The buyer's stated objection is 'we need to complete the Suez integration and decide the ERP platform first, THEN consider new tools' (1-2 year timeline). Challenge that sequencing with a pilot-first approach: a narrow, high-impact P2P pilot (on-time payment 75%→95%, supplier onboarding 20-30d→1wk) can run IN PARALLEL to the integration because Zycus is ERP-complementary (no SAP replacement required) and modular (deploy P2P independently, expand post-integration). Frame it as 'validate the AI ROI now on a contained use case so you can confidently scale it across the consolidated organization later, rather than wait 2 years and start from zero'. This is the ONLY path to pull the close date forward; otherwise, requalify to 2027-2028 pipeline. Position the pilot as a strategic hedge—if Ariba's automation pilots fail (as they have so far), Veolia needs a proven alternative ready to deploy when the integration completes.

deltas.to: Competitive displacement strategy vs Ariba: Ariba is the incumbent with low adoption and no successful AI pilots yet, but they have the operational-excellence t
deltas.subject: Competitive displacement strategy vs Ariba: Ariba is the incumbent with low adoption and no successful AI pilots yet, but they have the operational-excellence team's attention by default ('let's see what Ariba can do first'). Our counter: build a direct comparison narrative—'Ariba is a point-solution bolt-on with limited AI depth; Zycus is the comprehensive, AI-native source-to-pay platform with proven P2P automation (share 2-3 customer proof points: on-time payment improvement, supplier onboarding acceleration, tail-spend savings via ANA). Ariba will incrementally patch; Zycus leapfrogs.' Share this narrative WITH Ming/Josh as a Leave-Behind they can use internally, and offer a bake-off pilot: 'Run a 90-day Zycus P2P pilot alongside Ariba's RFQ automation test; measure which delivers measurable operational improvement.' This forces a data-driven decision and neutralizes the incumbent advantage. If Ariba's pilots continue to underwhelm, we become the obvious choice when budget reopens.

value.flag: Pilot-first wedge against the integration freeze: The buyer's stated objection is 'we need to complete the Suez integration and decide the ERP platform first, THEN consider new tools' (1-2 year timeline). Challenge that sequencing with a pilot-first approach: a narrow, high-impact P2P pilot (on-time payment 75%→95%, supplier onboarding 20-30d→1wk) can run IN PARALLEL to the integration because Zycus is ERP-complementary (no SAP replacement required) and modular (deploy P2P independently, expand post-integration). Frame it as 'validate the AI ROI now on a contained use case so you can confidently scale it across the consolidated organization later, rather than wait 2 years and start from zero'. This is the ONLY path to pull the close date forward; otherwise, requalify to 2027-2028 pipeline. Position the pilot as a strategic hedge—if Ariba's automation pilots fail (as they have so far), Veolia needs a proven alternative ready to deploy when the integration completes.

value.flag: Competitive displacement strategy vs Ariba: Ariba is the incumbent with low adoption and no successful AI pilots yet, but they have the operational-excellence team's attention by default ('let's see what Ariba can do first'). Our counter: build a direct comparison narrative—'Ariba is a point-solution bolt-on with limited AI depth; Zycus is the comprehensive, AI-native source-to-pay platform with proven P2P automation (share 2-3 customer proof points: on-time payment improvement, supplier onboarding acceleration, tail-spend savings via ANA). Ariba will incrementally patch; Zycus leapfrogs.' Share this narrative WITH Ming/Josh as a Leave-Behind they can use internally, and offer a bake-off pilot: 'Run a 90-day Zycus P2P pilot alongside Ariba's RFQ automation test; measure which delivers measurable operational improvement.' This forces a data-driven decision and neutralizes the incumbent advantage. If Ariba's pilots continue to underwhelm, we become the obvious choice when budget reopens.

Engage Josh to champion this pilot path internally.
